elements.sent-2411-001: Quint and Shubik (1997) conjectured that a non-degenerate $n\times n$ game has at most $2^n-1$ Nash equilibria in mixed strategies.
elements.sent-2411-002: The conjecture is true for $n\le 4$ but false for $n\ge6$.
elements.sent-2411-003: We answer it positively for the remaining case $n=5$, which had been open since 1999.
elements.sent-2411-004: The problem can be translated to a combinatorial question about the vertices of a pair of simple $n$-polytopes with $2n$ facets.
elements.sent-2411-005: We introduce a novel obstruction based on the index of an equilibrium, which states that equilibrium vertices belong to two equal-sized disjoint stable sets of the graph of the polytope.
elements.sent-2411-006: This bound is verified directly using the known classification of the 159,375 combinatorial types of dual neighborly polytopes in dimension 5 with 10 facets.
elements.sent-2411-007: Non-neighborly polytopes are analyzed with additional combinatorial techniques where the bound is used for their disjoint facets.
elements.sent-2411-008: A bimatrix game is a two-player game in strategic form, a basic model of game theory.
elements.sent-2411-009: Its central solution concept is Nash equilibrium, which is a pair of randomized strategies that are optimal against each other.
elements.sent-2411-010: Nash equilibria of bimatrix games are best understood as certain combinatorial properties of two polytopes derived from the payoff matrices of the two players, called best-response polytopes.
elements.sent-2411-011: The possible number of Nash equilibria is of interest for algorithms that find one or all Nash equilibria of a game.
elements.sent-2411-012: Most insights in this area have been found using properties and constructions of polytopes.
elements.sent-2411-013: The present paper continues in this tradition with additional new techniques.
elements.sent-2411-014: We answer a long-open problem: We show that a non-degenerate $5\times 5$ bimatrix game has at most 31 Nash equilibria (degenerate games are non-generic and may have infinitely many Nash equilibria).
elements.sent-2411-015: Throughout this paper, all the games that we talk about are assumed to be non-degenerate, which implies that their best-response polytopes are simple (defined by generic linear inequalities).
elements.sent-2411-016: Their evidence for this, not based on polytopes, was that this is the number of Nash equilibria in the ``coordination game'' where each player's payoff matrix is the identity matrix.
elements.sent-2411-017: The corresponding best-response polytope is the $n$-cube.
elements.sent-2411-018: For the non-degenerate games that we consider, every equilibrium strategy of a player is a separate polytope vertex, so that the number of vertices is an upper bound on the number of Nash equilibria.
elements.sent-2411-019: This implies the Quint-Shubik conjecture for $n\le 3$, and it was proved for $n=4$ by \cite{keiding-1997 and \cite{MP-1999 .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, the conjecture is false for $n\ge6$.
elements.sent-2411-021: Using dual cyclic polytopes, which for $n\times n$ games have proportional to about $2.6^n/\sqrt n$ many vertices, \cite{von-stengel-1999 constructed games with about $2.4^n/\sqrt n$ many Nash equilibria (slightly fewer for odd~$n$), and a $6\times 6$ game with 75 Nash equilibria as the first counterexample to the Quint-Shubik conjecture.}
elements.sent-2411-022: The remaining case $n=5$ had since been open, which we solve in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-023: We develop new techniques to obtain our result.
elements.sent-2411-024: Briefly, they are based on existing lists of combinatorial types of simple polytopes in dimensions 4 and 5 with 8, 9, and (for dual-neighborly polytopes) 10 facets, and a novel obstruction that limits the number of equilibrium vertices in certain face configurations.
elements.sent-2411-025: A basic version of this obstruction is that a triangle as a polytope face can have at most two equilibrium vertices, which ``loses'' one possible equilibrium vertex per triangle.
elements.sent-2411-026: This basic obstruction has been used by \cite{keiding-1997 to show that $4\times 4$ games have at most 15 Nash equilibria, using the list by \cite{gruenbaum-sreedharan-1967 of the 37 combinatorial types of simple polytopes in dimension~4 with up to 8 facets (3~of these 37 polytopes are dual neighborly, that is, any two facets intersect).}}
elements.sent-2411-027: For $5\times 5$ games, no such list is known.
elements.sent-2411-028: We make use of two lists by \cite{firsching-2017 , namely of the 159,375 dual neighborly simple polytopes in dimension~5 with 10 facets, and of all 1,142 simple polytopes in dimension~4 with 9 facets, but need further considerations.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Our novel obstruction is based on the concept of an equilibrium index due to \cite{shapley-1974 .}
elements.sent-2411-030: It is convenient to consider as an equilibrium also the ``artificial equilibrium'', given by the pair of all-zero vectors that are vertices of the two best-response polytopes and fulfill the equilibrium condition, but cannot be re-scaled as randomized strategies to represent a Nash equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-031: The important parity argument, based on the algorithm by \citet{LH and already exploited by \cite{keiding-1997 , states that the number of equilibria is even (and hence the number of Nash equilibria is odd).}}
elements.sent-2411-032: The index property strengthens this further: Half of the equilibria have index~$+1$, and the other half (including the artificial equilibrium) have index~$-1$.
elements.sent-2411-033: In a best-response polytope, equilibrium vertices connected by an edge have opposite index (Lemma~\ref{l:edge below).}
elements.sent-2411-034: Hence, in the graph of each best-response polytope (as defined by its vertices and edges), any two index $+1$ equilibrium vertices are non-adjacent and form a stable set (also called independent set), and the same applies to the index $-1$ equilibrium vertices.
elements.sent-2411-035: The maximum size of two disjoint stable sets of equal size is therefore an upper bound on the number of equilibria (Theorem~\ref{t:stable-sb ), which is our new stable-set bound.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Because the polytope graphs are small, this bound on equilibrium numbers can be computed quickly.
elements.sent-2411-037: For dual-neighborly polytopes, the bound of 32 equilibria (and hence 31 Nash equilibria) for a $5\times 5$ game can then be verified by computer calculations using the list by \cite{firsching-2017 of these 159,375 polytopes.}
elements.sent-2411-038: In fact, this result was already obtained in an earlier unpublished attempt by Vissarion Fisikopoulos and Bernhard von Stengel using the list of 159,750 neighborly oriented matroids by \cite{MiyataPadrol2015 , and using the simpler obstruction of disjoint simplices as faces, each of which can only contain two equilibrium vertices.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Oriented matroids are more general than polytopes (and hence the bound is valid), but only slightly so; \cite{firsching-2017 showed that all but 375 of them are realizable as polytopes.}
elements.sent-2411-040: The challenge solved in this paper is when a best-response polytope in a $5\times 5$ game is not dual neighborly and therefore has two disjoint facets.
elements.sent-2411-041: Such a polytope has at most 40 vertices (dual neighborly polytopes have 42 vertices).
elements.sent-2411-042: There is as yet no list of all 5-dimensional simple polytopes with 10 facets.
elements.sent-2411-043: The two disjoint facets are 4-dimensional simple polytopes with up to 8 facets.
elements.sent-2411-044: Each of these facets can be seen as a $4\times 4$ game, which has at most 16 equilibria.
elements.sent-2411-045: However, this does not complete the proof because there may be further equilibrium vertices on neither facet.
elements.sent-2411-046: A crucial observation is that the stable-set bound applies also to the equilibrium vertices on a facet of a best-response polytope (Theorem~\ref{t:facet , proved in a nicely combined use of polytopes and game theory).}
elements.sent-2411-047: Each of the two disjoint facets has one of the 37 combinatorial types known from \citet{gruenbaum-sreedharan-1967 .}
elements.sent-2411-048: By the new facet-stable-set bound, 35 of these types have at least 4 non-equilibrium vertices, and the entire polytope has most 40 vertices.
elements.sent-2411-049: The remaining two combinatorial types are the 4-cube and what we call the semi-cube (Figure~\ref{fi:semi-cube ).}
elements.sent-2411-050: In order to have more than 32 equilibria, at least one of the two disjoint facets is therefore the 4-cube or the semi-cube; furthermore, the corresponding facet of the other best-response polytope needs 9 facets as a polytope in dimension~4.
elements.sent-2411-051: Again, an exhaustive computer search of these 1,142 simple polytopes, and the stable-set bound, give combinatorial properties (Theorem \ref{t:4-9 ) that we use to show that more than 32 equilibria are not possible.}
elements.sent-2411-052: This completes the proof that $5\times 5$ games have no more than 31 Nash equilibria.
elements.sent-2411-053: For the history of using polyhedra for studying bimatrix games see \cite{von-stengel-2002 .}
elements.sent-2411-054: Cyclic polytopes have been used by \citet{SvS2006, SvS2016 to show that Lemke-Howson paths (see Section~\ref{s:LH ) may be exponentially long.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: The number of Nash equilibria in $N$-player games has been studied by \cite{mckelvey-mclennan-1997 and recently \cite{vujic-2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-056: The more restrictive rank-1 games were introduced by \cite{kannan-theobald-2010 and shown to have exponentially many equilibria in \cite{agm-2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-057: For semi-definite games, equilibrium numbers were considered by \cite{semidef-games and \cite{semidef-network-games , which are relevant for quantum games in \citep{bostanci-watrous-2021 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: The Quint-Shubik bound does hold for tropical games \citep{agm-2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-059: Section~\ref{s:prelim states preliminaries on bimatrix games on polytopes.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Best-response polytopes are recalled in Section~\ref{s:brp .}
elements.sent-2411-061: A concise definition of Lemke-Howson paths and the equilibrium index for our purposes is given in Section~\ref{s:LH .}
elements.sent-2411-062: The stable-set bound is proved in Section~\ref{s:stable .}
elements.sent-2411-063: Based on these concepts, Section~\ref{s:dim5 gives the main proof that non-degenerate $5 \times 5$ games have at most $31$ Nash equilibria.}
elements.section-2411-002: Preliminaries on bimatrix games and polytopes
elements.sent-2411-065: The transpose of a matrix $B$ is denoted by $B\T$.
elements.sent-2411-067: We treat vectors and scalars as matrices, so scalars are multiplied from the right with a column vector and from the left with a row vector.
elements.sent-2411-068: The $i$th component of a vector~$x$ is denoted by $x_i$.
elements.sent-2411-069: The all-zero vector is denoted by~$\0$ and the all-one vector by~$\1$, their dimension depending on the context.
elements.sent-2411-071: Inequalities between vectors such as $x\ge\0$ are assumed to hold for all components.
elements.sent-2411-072: We let $[k]=\{1,\ldots,k\}$ for any positive integer~$k$.
elements.sent-2411-073: An $m \times n$ bimatrix game is a pair of $m\times n$ matrices $(A,B)$.
elements.sent-2411-074: The $m$ rows and $n$ columns are the pure strategies of player 1 and~2, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-075: The players simultaneously choose a pure strategy pair, with the resulting entry of $A$ as payoff to player~1 and of $B$ to player~2.
elements.sent-2411-076: A mixed strategy is a probability vector over the player's pure strategies.
elements.sent-2411-077: Players are interested in maximizing their expected payoff, given by $x\T Ay$ to player~1 and $x\T By$ to player~2 if their mixed strategies are $x$ and~$y$.
elements.sent-2411-078: A mixed strategy is a best response to the other player's mixed strategy if it maximizes the player's expected payoff over all possible mixed strategies.
elements.sent-2411-079: A mixed-strategy pair of mutual best responses is called a Nash equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-080: For a mixed strategy, say $x$ of player~1, its support $\supp(x)$ is the set of pure strategies that are played with positive probability.
elements.sent-2411-081: It is easy to see that $x$ is a best response to a mixed strategy $y$ of player~2 if and only if every pure strategy in $\supp(x)$ is a best response to~$y$, that is, if the following best-response condition holds: \begin{equation}\label{br} x_i>0\quad\Rightarrow\quad (Ay)_i=u=\max_{k\in[m]} \,(Ay)_k \qquad(i\in[m]).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-082: If (\ref{br ) holds, then $u$ is also the best-response payoff $x\T Ay$ of the mixed strategy~$x$ against the mixed strategy~$y$.}
elements.sent-2411-083: The analogous condition holds for a mixed strategy~$y$ of player~2 being a best response to a mixed strategy~$x$ of player~1.
elements.sent-2411-084: A bimatrix game is non-degenerate if for any mixed strategy $z$ of a player there are at most $\lvert\supp(z)\rvert$ many pure strategies that are best responses to~$z$.
elements.sent-2411-085: For a comprehensive list of equivalent definitions see \citet[thm.~14]{vS21 .}
elements.sent-2411-086: Generic games $(A,B)$ (where the entries of $A$ and $B$ are chosen from some continuous distributions) are non-degenerate.
elements.sent-2411-088: We use the following concepts from convex geometry (see also \citealp{gruenbaum-2003,ziegler-1995,joswig-theobald-2013 ).}
elements.sent-2411-089: An affine combination of points $z^1,\ldots, z^k$ in some Euclidean space is of the form $\sum_{i=1 ^k z^i\lambda_i$ where $\lambda_1,\ldots,\lambda_k$ are reals with ${\sum_{i=1 ^k \lambda_i=1}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-090: It is called a convex combination if $\lambda_i\ge0$ for all~$i$.
elements.sent-2411-091: A set of points is convex if it is closed under forming convex combinations.
elements.sent-2411-092: The convex hull of a set of points is the smallest convex set that contains all these points.
elements.sent-2411-093: Given points are affinely independent if none of these points is an affine combination of the others.
elements.sent-2411-094: A convex set has dimension~$d$ if and only if it has $d+1$, but no more, affinely independent points.
elements.sent-2411-095: A polyhedron $P$ is a subset of $\reals^d$ defined by finitely many linear inequalities, that is, $P=\{z\in\reals^d\mid Cz\le q\}$ for some $C$ in $\reals^{\ell\times d}$ and some $q$~in~$\reals^\ell$.
elements.sent-2411-096: A face $F$ of the polyhedron $P$ is obtained by converting some of its inequalities into equalities, that is, if for some $S\subseteq \{1,\ldots,\ell\}$ \begin{equation}\label{face} F=\{z\in\reals^d\mid Cz\le q,~ (Cz)_i=q_i \hbox{ for }i\in S\}\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-097: A polytope is a bounded polyhedron.
elements.sent-2411-098: Suppose the polytope $P$ has dimension $d$ (also called a $d$-polytope; as a subset of $\reals^d$ it is then called full-dimensional).
elements.sent-2411-099: Then a face $F$ of $P$ is called a facet if it has dimension $d-1$, a ridge if it has dimension $d-2$, an edge if it has dimension $1$, and a vertex if it has dimension~$0$.
elements.sent-2411-100: A vertex is a singleton $\{v\}$ and also identified with~$v$.
elements.sent-2411-101: A $d$-polytope $P$ is called simple if every vertex of $P$ belongs to exactly $d$ facets.
elements.sent-2411-102: For the following more general statement see \citet[prop.~2.16(iv)]{ziegler-1995 .}
elements.sent-2411-103: In a simple $d$-polytope $P$, every non-empty face of $P$ of dimension $k$ belongs to exactly $d-k$ facets.
elements.sent-2411-104: The faces of a polytope, partially ordered by inclusion, form a lattice (see \citealp{ziegler-1995 ).}
elements.sent-2411-105: Two polytopes have the same combinatorial type if their face lattices are isomorphic.
elements.sent-2411-106: We denote the set of combinatorial types of $d$-dimensional simple polytopes with $k$ facets by $\mathcal{P _{k ^d$.}}
elements.sent-2411-107: A simple polytope is dual neighborly if any two of its facets have a non-empty intersection (which is then a ridge).
elements.sent-2411-108: For a given dimension and number of facets, dual neighborly polytopes have the largest numbers of vertices.
elements.sent-2411-109: The maximal number of vertices of a polytope in $\mathcal{P _{8 ^4$, $\mathcal{P _{9 ^4$, $\mathcal{P _{10 ^5$ is 20, 27, 42, respectively (see (3.2) in \citealp{joswig-theobald-2013 ).}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: The graph of a polytope is defined by the vertices and edges of the polytope, where in the graph an edge is considered as the unordered pair of its endpoints, written as $uv$ if the endpoints are $u$ and~$v$; then $u$ and $v$ are also called adjacent.
elements.sent-2411-111: The degree of a vertex in a graph is the number of vertices it is adjacent~to.
elements.sent-2411-112: For more details on the following construction of ``best-response polytopes'' see \cite{von-stengel-1999,von-stengel-2002,vS2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-113: Let $(A,B)$ be an $m\times n$ bimatrix game and consider the polyhedra \begin{equation}\label{PQ} \arraycolsep.2em \begin{array}{rcllrclrcl} P = \{~x \in \R^m \mid x \ge \0, B\T x \le \1~\}, Q = \{~y \in \R^n \mid A y \le \1, y \ge \0~\}. \end{array}\end{equation} We assume that $P$ and $Q$ are polytopes, which holds if and only if the best-response payoff to any mixed strategy of the other player is always positive \citep[lemma~9.9]{vS2022 .}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\label{PQ} \arraycolsep.2em {rcllrclrcl} P = \{~x \in \R^m \mid x \ge \0, B\T x \le \1~\}, Q = \{~y \in \R^n \mid A y \le \1, y \ge \0~\}. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-114: A sufficient condition for this is that $A$ and $B\T $ are nonnegative and have no zero column.
elements.sent-2411-115: This is not restrictive since adding a constant to every entry of a payoff matrix does not change best responses.
elements.sent-2411-116: With the exception of $\0$, every $x$ in $P$ represents a mixed strategy of player~1 after re-scaling it to $x\frac1{\1\T x}$ as a probability distribution.
elements.sent-2411-117: Similarly, every $y$ in $Q\setminus\{\0\}$ represents a mixed strategy $y\frac1{\1\T y}$ of player~2.
elements.sent-2411-118: We always consider $x$ in $P\setminus\{\0\}$ and $y$ in $Q\setminus\{\0\}$ as ``mixed strategies'' with this implied re-scaling.
elements.sent-2411-119: Suppose that $j\in[n]$ and consider the last $n$ inequalities $B\T x\le\1$ in the definition of~$P$ in (\ref{PQ ).}
elements.sent-2411-120: If the $j$th inequality is binding (holds as equality), that is, $(B\T x)_j=1$, then $j$ is a pure best response of player~2 to~$x$.
elements.sent-2411-121: That is, the right-hand side~$1$ in every inequality in $B\T x\le\1$ represents the best-response payoff to~$x$, re-scaled as~$1$, if at least one of these inequalities is binding; the actual payoff is the re-scaling factor $\frac1{\1\T x}$.
elements.sent-2411-122: Similarly, a binding inequality $(Ay)_i=1$ for $i\in[m]$ among the inequalities $Ay\le\1$ in the definition of~$Q$ means that $i$ is a pure best response to~$y$.
elements.sent-2411-123: If $x=\0$ then $B\T x<\1$ and if $y=\0$ then $Ay<\1$.
elements.sent-2411-124: Hence, $P$ and $Q$ contain the vector $\1\eps$ in $\reals^m$ respectively $\reals^n$ for sufficiently small positive~$\eps$ and are full-dimensional.
elements.sent-2411-125: We introduce labels in $[m+n]$ to uniquely identify the pure strategies of player~1 as $1,\ldots,m$ and of player~2 as $m+1,\ldots,m+n$.
elements.sent-2411-126: The inequalities in (\ref{PQ ) are written in the order of these labels.}
elements.sent-2411-127: For a point $x\in P$ or~$y\in Q$, its labels are the labels of the binding inequalities for that point.
elements.sent-2411-128: That is, for a pure strategy $i$ in $[m]$ of player~1, the point $x$ has label~$i$ if $x_i=0$, and $y$ has label~$i$ if $i$ is a best response to the mixed strategy~$y$ (if $y=\0$ then $Ay<\1$ and $y$ cannot have label~$i$).
elements.sent-2411-129: Similarly, for a pure strategy $j$ in $[n]$ of player~2, the mixed strategy~$x$ has label~$m+j$ if $j$ is a best response to~$x$, and $y$ has label~$m+j$ if $y_j=0$.
elements.sent-2411-130: We also consider these labels if $x=\0$ (then $x$ has labels $1,\ldots,m$) or $y=\0$ (then $y$ has labels $m+1,\ldots,m+n$).
elements.sent-2411-131: By the best-response condition~(\ref{br ), $(x,y)$ is a Nash equilibrium of $(A,B)$ if and only if every pure strategy of a player is a best response or played with probability zero (or both).}
elements.sent-2411-132: That is, every label in $[m+n]$ has to appear as a label of $x$ or~$y$.
elements.sent-2411-133: Consider a bimatrix game $(A,B)$ and assume that $P$ and $Q$ in $(\ref{PQ )$ are polytopes, called the best-response polytopes for the game.}
elements.sent-2411-134: Let $(x,y)\in P\times Q$ be such that every element of $[m+n]$ appears as a label of $x$ or~$y$.
elements.sent-2411-135: Then $(x,y)$ is called completely labeled and an equilibrium of the game.
elements.sent-2411-137: Any other equilibrium, with $x$ and $y$ re-scaled as mixed strategies, is a Nash equilibrium of $(A,B)$.
elements.sent-2411-138: Note that if $x\in P\setminus\{\0\}$, then $x$ is missing at least one label $i\in[m]$, which in an equilibrium $(x,y)$ has to appear as a label of $y$ and therefore $y\ne\0$.
elements.sent-2411-139: Hence $(\0,\0)$ is the only equilibrium $(x,y)$ where $x=\0$ or $y=\0$.
elements.sent-2411-140: The Nash equilibria $(x,y)$ of a bimatrix game are exactly the completely labeled pairs of points in $P\times Q\setminus \{(\0,\0)\}$, even for a degenerate game.
elements.sent-2411-141: For a non-degenerate game, they are always pairs of vertices.
elements.sent-2411-142: For a bimatrix game $(A,B)$ with best-response polytopes $P$ and $Q$ , the following are equivalent: \rmitem{(a) The game is non-degenerate.}
elements.sent-2411-143: Furthermore, assume the game is non-degenerate and let $(x,y)\in P\times Q$ be an equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-144: Conditions (b) and (c) are theorem~14 (b) and (h) of \cite{vS21 .}
elements.sent-2411-145: Because there are only $m+n$ labels in total, (b) implies~(d).
elements.sent-2411-146: In Lemma~\ref{l:nondeg (c), redundant inequalities refer to the description of the polytope, which may not affect whether the polytope as a set is simple.}
elements.sent-2411-147: It implies, for example, that there are no duplicate inequalities.
elements.sent-2411-148: A never-binding inequality represents a pure strategy that is never a best response; this does not affect non-degeneracy and may exist in a generic game, but we can omit such a strategy from the game.
elements.sent-2411-149: In the non-degenerate games that we consider, every binding inequality defines a facet that has its own label.
elements.sent-2411-150: The transition from bimatrix games to polytopes can also be reversed.
elements.sent-2411-151: For projective transformations see \citet{ziegler-1995 , of which (\ref{proj ) is a simple form.}}
elements.sent-2411-152: Let $(x_0,y_0)$ be a completely labeled vertex pair of $P\times Q$.
elements.sent-2411-153: Then there is a game $(A',B')$ with best-response polytopes $P'$ and $Q'$ such that: \rmitem{(a) There are affine bijections $P\to P'$ and $Q\to Q'$ with a corresponding permutation of $[m+n]$ that maps the facet labels of $P$ and $Q$ to those of $P'$ and $Q'$, with $(x_0,y_0)$ mapped to $(\0,\0)$ in $P'\times Q'$, such that all completely labeled vertex pairs of $P\times Q$ are in bijection to those of $P'\times Q'$.}
elements.sent-2411-155: For (b), note that e.g.\ $Q'$, given by $\{y'\in\reals^n\mid A'y'\le\1,~y'\ge\0\}$, may be a polytope even when $A'$ has non-positive entries.
elements.sent-2411-156: In that case add a sufficiently positive constant $\alpha$ to all entries of $A'$ to yield an all-positive matrix $\hat A=A'+\1\alpha\1\T$, and let $\hat Q= \{\hat y\in\reals^n\mid \hat A\hat y\le\1,~\hat y\ge\0\}$.
elements.sent-2411-157: We claim that \begin{equation}\label{proj} y'\mapsto \hat y = y'\frac{1}{1+\alpha\,\1\T y'}\end{equation} is a bijective projective transformation $Q'\to\hat Q$ that preserves all binding inequalities and therefore the face lattices of $Q'$ and~$\hat Q$.
elements.sent-2411-158: In (\ref{proj ), $\0$ in $Q'$ is mapped to $\0$ in $\hat Q$, and for $y'\ne\0$ the mapping in (\ref{proj ) is obtained by mapping $y'$ to $(\overline y,u)$ with a mixed strategy $\overline y=y'u$ where $u=\frac1{\1\T y'}$, then $(\overline y,u)$ to $(\overline y,u+\alpha)$, and this is mapped to $\hat y=\overline y\frac1{u+\alpha}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-159: To see that binding inequalities are preserved in $Q'$ and~$\hat Q$, clearly $y'_j=0$ if and only if $\hat y_j=0$ for $j\in[n]$.
elements.sent-2411-160: Furthermore, $\hat A\hat y\le\1$ if and only if $\hat A y'\le \1(1+\alpha\1\T y')$, which for the $i$th row $a_i\T$ of $A'$ means $ (a_i\T + \alpha \1\T)y'\le 1+\alpha\1\T y' $ or equivalently $a_i\T y'\le 1$, which is the $i$th inequality in $A'y'\le\1$.
elements.sent-2411-161: A similar transformation changes $B'$ to an all-positive matrix $\hat B$ and $P'$ to a ``projectively equivalent'' polytope~$\hat P$.
elements.sent-2411-162: Proposition~\ref{p:biject allows changing the labels of $P$ and $Q$ such that a Nash equilibrium and the artificial equilibrium exchange roles, which we will make use of in Theorem~\ref{t:facet .}}
elements.section-2411-004: Equilibria come in pairs of opposite index
elements.sent-2411-163: Our ``stable-set bound'' is based on the fact that equilibria come in pairs of opposite index, which we explain in this section.
elements.sent-2411-164: The algorithm by \cite{LH starts at one equilibrium by following a path of alternating edges in the best-response polytopes $P$ and $Q$ in (\ref{PQ ) where one of the labels is allowed to be missing, until it terminates at another equilibrium.}}
elements.sent-2411-165: The endpoints of the Lemke-Howson paths are equilibria of opposite index.
elements.sent-2411-166: The most concise description, given here, considers the product polytope $Z=P\times Q$.
elements.sent-2411-167: For more details see, for example, \citet{von-stengel-2002,vS21,vS2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-168: A labeled polytope $Z$ is a simple $d$-polytope of the form \begin{equation}\label{labpol} Z =\{z\in \reals^d\mid -z\le\0,~ Cz\le \1 \}\end{equation} for some $k\times d$ matrix $C$ without redundant inequalities where each binding inequality has a label in $[d]$, such that: For $i\in[d]$, the $i$th inequality $-z_i\le0$ in $-z\le\0$ has label~$i$, and for $j\in[k]$, the $j$th inequality $(Cz)_j\le 1$ in $Cz\le\1$ has some given label $\ell(j)$ in $[d]$.
elements.sent-2411-169: A point $z$ in $Z$ that has all labels in $[d]$ is called completely labeled, and $h$-almost completely labeled if it has all labels in $[d]\setminus\{h\}$, for some $h\in[d]$.
elements.sent-2411-170: We will apply this to $Z=P\times Q$ where in Definition~\ref{d:labpol we take $d=m+n$, $z=(x,y)$, and \begin{equation}\label{CAB} C=\left[\begin{matrix}0 A B\T 0\end{matrix}\right], \quad k=d, \quad\hbox{and}\quad \ell(j)=j ~~\hbox{ for }j\in[k]\,.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-171: Let $Z$ be the labeled polytope as in Definition~\ref{d:labpol .}
elements.sent-2411-172: Because no inequality is redundant, every facet is defined by a binding inequality and has a unique label.
elements.sent-2411-173: Every vertex of $Z$ belongs to exactly $d$ facets and is incident to exactly $d$ edges, obtained by allowing one of the binding inequalities to be non-binding (visualized as ``moving away'' from the respective facet).
elements.sent-2411-174: Consider a fixed label $h$ in $[d]$ that is allowed to be ``missing'', and all the points in $Z$ that are $h$-almost completely labeled.
elements.sent-2411-175: Those points that have each label in $[d]\setminus\{h\}$ exactly once define edges of $Z$ except for the endpoints of these edges.
elements.sent-2411-176: Their endpoints, which are vertices of $Z$, are either completely labeled, or are missing label $h$ and therefore have a duplicate label.
elements.sent-2411-177: Any such vertex with the duplicate label belongs to two facets that have that label, and is incident to two $h$-almost completely labeled edges, which belong to only one of the two facets.
elements.sent-2411-178: Hence, the $h$-almost completely labeled points of $Z$ are vertices and edges of $Z$ that define a subgraph of the graph of $Z$ where every vertex has either degree two (with a duplicate label but missing label~$h$) or degree one (if the vertex is completely labeled).
elements.sent-2411-179: Such a graph consists of paths and cycles, and every path has two endpoints, which are the completely labeled vertices, so their number is even.
elements.sent-2411-180: Completely labeled vertices (except $\0$) of a labeled polytope also represent Nash equilibria of a game, namely of the $d\times k$ game $(U,C\T)$ where $U=[e_{\ell(1)}\cdots e_{\ell(k) ]$ is composed of the unit vectors for the labels of the rows of~$C$.}
elements.sent-2411-181: This simplifies certain constructions of games by using only a single labeled polytope, see \cite{SvS2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-182: Applied to $C$ in (\ref{CAB ), $U$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix, whose Nash equilibria $(z,w)$ correspond to the symmetric Nash equilibria $(z,z)$ of the symmetric game $(C,C\T)$, which we seek when setting $z=(x,y)$ to find the Nash equilibria $(x,y)$ of $(A,B)$.}
elements.sent-2411-183: Consider a labeled $d$-polytope $Z$ as in Definition~\ref{d:labpol and a completely labeled vertex $z$ with its $d$ binding inequalities written as $a_i\T z=\beta_i$ for each label $i\in[d]$.}
elements.sent-2411-184: That is, if the $i$th inequality in $-z\le\0$ is binding then $a_i\T z=\beta_i$ stands for $-z_i=0$ with $a_i$ as the negative $-e_i$ of the $i$th unit vector~$e_i$, and if $i=\ell(j)$ then the $j$th inequality in $Cz\le\1$ has label~$i$ and $a_i\T z=\beta_i$ stands for $(Cz)_j=1$ with $a_i\T$ as the $j$th row of~$C$.
elements.sent-2411-185: Writing the facet normal vectors $a_1,\ldots,a_d$ in the order of their labels as a matrix with determinant $|a_1\cdots a_d|$, we define the index of $z$ as the sign of this determinant (times $-1$ if $d$ is even), \begin{equation}\label{index} \ix(z)=(-1)^{d+1}\sign(|a_1\cdots a_d|).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-186: Because the polytope $Z$ is simple, the normal vectors $a_i$ of the incident facets of a vertex are linearly independent and the determinant is non-zero.
elements.sent-2411-187: In (\ref{index ), the minus sign for even $d$ has the purpose of ensuring that the artificial equilibrium~$\0$ has index $-1$, because the determinant of the negative of the $d\times d$ identity matrix is $(-1)^d$.}

elements.sent-2411-324: Let $P$ and $Q$ be non-neighborly, with disjoint facets $P_1$ and $P_2$ of $P$, and let $P_1$ be a semi-cube.
elements.sent-2411-325: Assume that the game has 34 or more equilibria (their number is even by Theorem~\ref{t:even ), which will lead to a contradiction.}
elements.sent-2411-326: The stable-set set bound for $P_1$ is 14 by (\ref{37 ), and $P_1$ needs to have 14 equilibrium vertices, by Lemma~\ref{l:ruleout3 .}}
elements.sent-2411-327: The remaining 20 equilibrium vertices with label~1 belong to~$Q_1$, which is in $\mathcal P_9^4$ by Theorem~\ref{t:4-9 (a) (by (\ref{37 ), no polytope in $\mathcal P_8^4$ has 20 equilibrium vertices).}}
elements.sent-2411-328: Hence, $Q_1$ intersects with every other facet of $Q$, where the resulting ridge (a three-dimensional face both of~$Q$ and of any facet of~$Q$) is never a 3-cube by Theorem~\ref{t:4-9 (c).}
elements.sent-2411-329: Hence, none of the facets of~$Q$ is a 4-cube.
elements.sent-2411-330: In order to obtain more than 32 equilibria, the two disjoint facets of $Q$ are therefore semi-cubes, because combining a semi-cube with any of the other combinatorial types in (\ref{37 ) would give at least $3+4$ obstruction vertices.}
elements.sent-2411-331: This argument with the polytopes exchanged implies that $P_2$ is also a semi-cube.
elements.sent-2411-333: By Theorem~\ref{t:4-9 (d), both $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ have 20 equilibrium vertices and at least five obstruction vertices.}
elements.sent-2411-334: By Theorem~\ref{t:4-9 (d) and (b), each of the other eight facets of $Q$ has at least three obstruction vertices.}
elements.sent-2411-335: Counted by multiplicity over all ten facets (each vertex belongs to five facets), the number of obstruction vertices is therefore at least $(2\cdot 5+8\cdot 3)/5=34/5>6$.
elements.sent-2411-336: Hence, $Q$ has at least seven obstruction vertices, but at most 40 vertices, and therefore at most 32 equilibria.
elements.sent-2411-337: Due to the symmetry of the role of the two polytopes, case (ii) and the previous lemma cover the case that at least one of the disjoint facet pairs of $P$ or $Q$ contains a semi-cube.
elements.sent-2411-338: The remaining case (i) is covered by the following lemma.
elements.sent-2411-339: Let $P$ and $Q$ be non-neighborly and assume that one of the disjoint facets $P_1,P_2$ is 4-cube, and that one facet of $Q$ is a 4-cube.
elements.sent-2411-341: Assume that the game has 34 or more equilibria, which will lead to a contradiction.
elements.sent-2411-342: If $P_1$ had 15 or fewer equilibrium vertices, then the remaining equilibria have label~1 with their 19 equilibrium vertices in $Q_1$, which needs to have a stable-set bound of 20 but then has no 3-cube as a facet by Theorem~\ref{t:4-9 (c).}
elements.sent-2411-343: However, then $Q$ has no 3-cube as a ridge, which contradicts $Q$ having a 4-cube as a facet.
elements.sent-2411-344: Hence, all 16 vertices of $P_1$ are equilibrium vertices.
elements.sent-2411-345: The 4-cube $P_1$ has a special combinatorial structure, which is isomorphic to that of the unit 4-cube $W=\{z\in\reals^4\mid 0\le z_i\le 1$ for $i\in[4]\}$.
elements.sent-2411-346: Each of the eight inequalities of $W$ corresponds to a facet of $P_1$ with a label in $\{3,\ldots,10\}$.
elements.sent-2411-347: These facets of $P_1$ come in four pairs with labels $g_i,h_i$ that correspond to the binding inequalities $z_i=0$ and $z_i=1$ in~$W$ for $i\in[4]$.
elements.sent-2411-348: Every vertex $v$ of $P_1$ has exactly one label $g_i$ or $h_i$ for $i\in[4]$, and has a complementary vertex (diagonally opposite in~$W$) defined by exactly the other labels from each pair.
elements.sent-2411-349: Then the {partner of $v$ in $Q$ has these four complementary labels, as well as label~2, so all the partners of $P_1$ are in~$Q_2$.}
elements.sent-2411-350: The complementary vertices in $P_1$ themselves have also their partners in~$Q_2$.
elements.sent-2411-351: The resulting labels show that the partners of all the vertices of $P_1$ represent a 4-cube in~$Q_2$.
elements.sent-2411-352: In particular, all of them have four neighbors (via their edges) in $Q_2$.
elements.sent-2411-353: Because $Q_2$ is a simple 4-polytope, it does not have any further vertices because they could not be connected to these 16 vertices.
elements.sent-2411-354: Hence, $Q_2$ is itself a 4-cube and has exactly 16 equilibrium vertices.
elements.sent-2411-355: Every equilibrium $(x,y)$ has to have label~2.
elements.sent-2411-357: The other equilibria $(x,y)$ require that $x\in P_2$, where $P_2\in\mathcal P_8^4$, but $P_2$ has at most 16 equilibrium vertices by~(\ref{37 ).}
elements.sent-2411-358: Lemmas~\ref{l:ruleout3 ,~\ref{l:semi , and~\ref{l:16facets cover the cases (iii), (ii), (i) of the pairs $P_1,P_2$ of disjoint facets of $P$ that together have fewer than seven obstruction vertices.}}}
elements.sent-2411-359: This completes the proof of Theorem~\ref{t:5x5 .}
